title: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
concept: QAT simulates quantization errors during training, letting the model adjust its weights to mitigate precision loss before final export.

exercises.prompt: Why do developers want to quantize (compress) AI models?

options.text: To make the model smaller and faster on mobile devices
To make the model take up more storage space
To slow down the model's response speed

exercises.explain: Quantization is the process of reducing the precision of numbers (e.g., from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers) to make models smaller and faster.

cards.title: Train for the Brain Shrink
cards.body: When we shrink AI models to fit on phones, we usually round their numbers to save space. But doing this after training can make the AI lose its train of thought! What if we trained the AI to expect these rough roundings from the very start?

cards.title: What is QAT?
cards.body: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) is a method where we simulate the math errors caused by using lower-precision numbers (like 8-bit integers) during the training phase. By letting the model see and feel these rounding errors as it learns, it can adjust its weights to prevent accuracy drops when it is finally compressed.

cards.title: The Simple-Speech Practice
cards.body: Imagine writing down a speech using only a few basic words instead of your normal vocabulary. If you suddenly do this right before going on stage, you will sound choppy and confuse your audience. QAT is like practicing your speech out loud using only those simple words ahead of time. You learn to express your ideas perfectly within the new limits before the big performance.

cards.title: QAT in Action
cards.body: When training a mobile image detector, a developer turns on QAT. During training, a special layer simulates how pixel values will look when rounded from 32-bit floats down to 8-bit integers. If a rounding error causes a misclassification, the training loop corrects the weight parameters. When deployed, the compressed model detects objects almost as accurately as the massive original!

cards.title: Myth: Compression Must Ruin Accuracy
cards.body: People often think that compressing a model always degrades its intelligence. While post-training compression can cause errors, QAT proves that if you train the model with these limits in mind, it can recover almost all of its original performance!

cards.title: PTQ vs. QAT
cards.body: Compare how Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) and Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) handle the transition to lower precision.

left.text: Model is trained in high precision, then rounded. This sudden shock often causes a drop in accuracy.

right.text: Model practices with simulated rounding errors during training. It adapts its weights to maintain accuracy.

exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of using Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) over simple post-training quantization?

options.text: It helps maintain higher accuracy by letting the model adapt to precision loss during training.
It makes the training process run ten times faster.
It completely eliminates the need for any compression tools.
It increases the physical size of the model file.

exercises.explain: QAT helps the model learn to tolerate and bypass the loss of detail caused by rounding, preserving high performance on smaller devices.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Quantization-Aware Training simulates rounding errors during the training process rather than applying them only at the end.
exercises.explain: QAT specifically simulates quantization errors during training so that the weights can adapt to the lower precision limits before export.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how QAT prepares a model for compression.
exercises.sentence: During training, QAT _ quantization errors so the model can adjust its weights.
exercises.explain: QAT works by simulating the rounding errors that happen in lower precision, allowing the model to adapt before final deployment.

exercises.prompt: Which description represents a model undergoing Quantization-Aware Training?

options.text: Special 'fake quantization' nodes are added to the training graph to model rounding errors.
The fully trained, high-precision model is converted to 8-bit integers directly without retraining.
The model is trained entirely with normal 32-bit floating point numbers and left uncompressed.

exercises.explain: The option describing fake quantization nodes represents QAT, which inserts simulation errors into the training graph. The other options describe Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) or standard training.

exercises.prompt: You are preparing a large language model to run on a smartphone using Quantization-Aware Training (QAT). How does QAT ensure the model stays accurate despite using less memory?

options.text: It simulates low-precision rounding errors during training, while updating weights using high-precision math.
It trains the model normally in 32-bit float and truncates the values at the very end without any adjustment.
It uses pure integer math on CPU hardware for training from start to finish.
It disables backpropagation entirely to prevent weights from changing during quantization.

exercises.explain: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) simulates the rounding errors of lower precision (like 8-bit integers) while updating weights using floating-point math during training. This lets the model adapt to the errors so it remains highly accurate after final conversion!
